speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] interviewing Mr. Nicholas Gardos, in Sidney, the 24th of May 1995. And this is tape five. The family, the mishpokhe. Mr. Gardos, would you do me the pleasure of interviewing your lovely family?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That is really a pleasure. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my wife Ruth. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We've been married for 38 years, a little bit over. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my daughter, Julie, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my son-in-law, Gavin-- Gavin Fisher. Mr. and Mrs. Fisher. This is my son, Robbie. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And last but not least-- Maxie-- this is my grandson, Maxie. Max Fisher.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something about your family on camera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] all I can say is that I'm a very lucky man to have a family like this. And [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I think I really must have done something good to deserve a family like this. We--

speaker: interviewee
text: Give him back. Didn't work. OK. You want to go to Daddy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] don't know what one is supposed to be saying about family. But, you know, in Yiddish there's a saying that that [YIDDISH], that a face gives away the story. So I don't know what you can see in the face of my family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If you look at my face, all I can say is [YIDDISH]. And I'm grateful to have a family after all what happened. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my life is only worth living because I have a family like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't ask for the squeaky grandson did you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gardos, could you tell us about your liberation? Where it happened and when.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was liberated on the 30th of April, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were liberated in transit. We set out from Muehldorf. We were put into the usual transportation, into the cattle trucks, to be transported. And without any destination. Some people say that we were supposed to have been shot into the Starnberger See, where we actually arrived there. Some people differ so I can't give you exact information about it, what our fate was supposed to be. But our transport [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] consisted of approximately 15, 16 cattle trucks. And on the end of the train there were two platform-- two open trucks, which had anti-aircraft [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] battery on them. Unfortunately for us, because their first experience was-- when we arrived to Munich after a couple of days. We arrived to Munich, and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] during our stay on the Munich railway station, there was an air attack by the Allies. And, of course, they didn't know-- first of all, they didn't care what they shot at. They wanted to destroy the railway station. And secondly, if they saw it at all, they watched it all, they saw the two anti-aircraft batteries attached to the truck. So, naturally, they went for the truck. We had a lot of casualties. The wagon I was in, nobody got injured and nobody died. The others, I heard, they had casualties, they had dead. After that-- that attack came from, not from bombers, but also from the twin-bodied aircraft, which came down very, very low and just machine gunned. Whatever they could see. But after the experience in Munich, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we started to-- they pushed us back and forwards-- and really we didn't know where we were going. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We arrived to the Starnberger See, which is south of Munich. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was after five days. It took us five days. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Our food consisted of water and carrots and dried vegetables. In actual fact, we were not starving. We were hungry. We didn't have enough food. Definitely did not have enough food. They didn't get any food. We had big bags of carrots and dried food put into a wagon when we started out. The only times they opened up the wagon was when we arrived to a place called Gauting. Gauting is also a railway junction. And the reason why they opened up our trucks was because there was an announcement from the station master, the war has ended. The war is over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our guards they opened up the railway trucks and everybody got off and everybody was running in every direction. Some people took to the hills, which was just on one side of the railway track. And some people went into town to get their hands on food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow, I don't know why, a few of us who kept together, we didn't move away. We just stayed. We just stayed around the wagon. We stretched our legs. We walked around. We didn't have much strength to-- by the way, I certainly didn't have any strength, because I just recovered from typhus, from Fleck typhus. And that was another miracle, because we were supposed to have marched like all the other transfers before us marched from Muehldorf. And maybe because time was running out-- I had Fleck typhus in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] end of March, beginning of April. And the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] only reason why I survived was because Dr. Lieberman kept coming into the cordoned off part of the camp, where all the typhus cases were, and kept bringing me food, and whatever medication he could. But, at the same time, I had on my left foot, I had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a sort of-- what do you call it-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a wound, which had to be cut open. He cut it open for me. He cut it open secretly-- and we're undercover-- and put as much of the infection on it as possible and drained it. Because it probably was the beginning of what my father had, whatever it was, I had it only in one place. That's right on my foot here, on the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I could not possibly have put any shoe on. And I couldn't have marched. There was no way that I could have walked. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the typhus I certainly had no strength to walk. Even if that wouldn't have been there. So I certainly kept then-- as I say, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I certainly kept to the place, and I didn't go to the hills and I didn't go to town. And a few other people also stayed with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And within about an hour, there was a whole complete turn around, because the war wasn't finished. The station master made a mistake, he misunderstood the announcement. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The news came that it was a mistake, and everybody back into the trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the meantime, while this was happening, we had another air attack. And the pilots saw people running all around, but they also saw the two anti-aircraft batteries on the end of the train. So they started to machine gun the whole place down. And then we had a lot of deads in that spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: People who run to the hills, a lot of them were shot by mistake. The rest, they were shot. So the people who went looting, some of them came back, some of them didn't come back. I don't know. But I know that we went back into the train. We were locked up again. And the train moved away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were on the way to the Starnberger See. And one morning, very, very early-- it was just sunrise-- we heard tanks rambling, you know? We didn't know what it was. I mean, tanks were tanks. And we guessed they were tanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, all of a sudden, we heard people shouting-- German shouting-- we heard. All of a sudden we heard English, English speaking. Then we knew already that, you know, the news is definitely good news. They opened up the-- this time they opened up the wagons. And this time it was a real liberation. It was the Seventh Army who liberated us. And all I can tell you is that the site, which I didn't expect, was we had a number of German SS with their throat cut lying next to the railway line. And what happened was, which I learned afterwards, that we had also in the camp we had some Greek Jews. Those Greek Jews were in camp for almost four years. And they were really and truly like hardened criminals, because they stole the food from us, they stole-- they did everything to survive. They didn't care. They had no scruples. In the camp, in [INAUDIBLE], if you went to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the bathroom, which was a long barrack with 50 people in it, and you put your soap down, and you turned, and you didn't keep your eye on it, you could be sure it was gone within seconds. You put-- your bread, your ration, you can only keep under your head when you slept. And then it wasn't safe. And every time it came back it was the Greeks. The Greeks every time. I mean, you couldn't-- they were more on an animal level than we were. I don't know if they would have killed for a piece of bread, but they definitely didn't stop at anything. And that's why they survived. Anyhow, these Greeks, as it turned out to be, they were the ones a few wagons down from us. When the American soldiers opened up the doors, they jumped off, they took the bayonets from the Germans, just straight away. Until-- the Americans didn't know-- they were not fast enough to stop them. Or maybe they didn't want to stop them, I don't know. But they were quite a number. Five or six or seven, I didn't know. They were lying there, just their throat cut. Simple as that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was liberation. Of course, liberation wasn't quite like that, because I can't describe the joy. I can't-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't think I can describe the feeling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Even after 50 years. It's something which it-- well, if somebody is sentenced to death, and he faces death 100 times, and all of sudden they say you can live. I think it's not an unnatural reaction. So we went. We walked around, and went down. And then we went down to the [NON-ENGLISH], which, the highway where all the tanks came by the hundred. And the soldiers, of course-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I think there were a lot of Jews so it was-- Did any of them speak to you at all Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't. I can only tell you that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] those soldiers-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] they just wanted to kill every German they could put their hands on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But, you know, the joy [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of being free [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and being told by a person in uniform that I'm also a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That is the one which put [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] faith back into one, you know? There is another world. There is a world where Jews are still not below animal level. And, you know, to be liberated by Jewish soldiers was really, absolutely miraculous. So, anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after that we learned-- I am sorry to be so emotional about it, but [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to some people it doesn't matter, but to me it did matter. I actually saw my fellow-- my people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, what happened was that the German commandant who was in charge of the train, he was awarded by the Allies. He was kept-- he was given special treatment, because it was him who saved the whole transport. He juggled back and forwards the transport, from one place to the other place, back and forward, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until the Allies came-- until the Americans came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I know our camp commandant was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sturmbannfuhrer [INAUDIBLE]. Sturmbannfuhrer [INAUDIBLE]. But I don't know the name of the commandant. And I must say I tried to find that from people who with me on the transport, and they don't know it either. They don't know it either. I just met a person the other day. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I never knew he was in Muehldorf, and I never knew that he was in the same transport as I was. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It's strange. Anyway, that was the place where we were liberated was called Tutzing. Tutzing is also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a little place. And then we were taken to Feldafing. Feldafing is one of the places where thousands of people were taken and looked after. It was a place where the German-- where the SS had there [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE] camp and sanatorium. And we were treated very, very well. And, actually, we were given every possible attention and brought back to life. Unfortunately, those people who went looting and got their hands on food, they didn't survive because the body couldn't take it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the last minute, on the doorstep of freedom, they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were liberated. We were in Feldafing. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And stayed there until the Palestinian brigade came and started to do a bit of propaganda and asking the young people to come to Israel. And they came two or three times until I decided myself, amongst a number of other people, to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because one thing was certain in my mind. I never wanted to set foot in Hungary again. That I had no hesitation about. No, I didn't forget the speech. You are being taken away, never to be returned. But the reason, one of the reasons that I didn't want to go back, that transport came through from all over Germany, through Feldafing. And we had one transport which came through, women. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] They stopped for maybe an hour, and they were taken somewhere else, because there were many camps around there at the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I happened to meet some women who I recognized from home. And naturally what does one do first? One asking about family. And I asked about my mother and my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Turned out to be that these women came and were in Bergen-Belsen. And my mother and sister also were in Bergen-Belsen with them. That was the first time I heard that they were in Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they told me that, unfortunately, don't expect your mother and your sister back, because we were together. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] If I would have had any doubt at all whatsoever where to go, I had no doubt, you know, no doubt in my mind at all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, end of July, beginning of August again-- sorry that was April, April the 30th. Yes, it was the beginning of July. Beginning of July, we were put in trucks and under cover, we were transported though by the [INAUDIBLE], by the Palestinian Brigade, to Italy, to Treviso. Why under cover? Because we were Aliyah Bet, which is illegal. And the English didn't want to let us go through. So until we got to Italy, we had to stay in covered trucks. And they sort of smuggled us through the border. Wasn't any danger. What could have happened? But nevertheless, it was illegal. How and when did you find out that your mother and sister were actually alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- how did I find out? I was waiting for my ship to arrive to take me to Israel. And I was in Italy for practically a whole year, going from one of these [NON-ENGLISH] or camps to the other one. And we were near Naples at that time. And again, from the Palestinian Brigade, I met somebody who was from our neighborhood, who went to Israel, to Palestine, years and years ago and joined the English Army as a Palestinian Brigade. And he was one of the people in Italy who fought during the war. And this one speaks to one other. I didn't recognize him, because I didn't know him. But we asked each of the questions, like everybody else. It's only questions, questions, and more questions. And it turned out to be that he was from hometown. It turned to be that the families have known each other for the last two generations and whatever. So I said to him, look, his name was King, Irving King. So they had a printer at home. His family had a printer at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, if you go home, have a look who is alive. I didn't know who was alive, who wasn't alive or whatever. I told you now I only learned afterwards, naturally. So he came back after three or four weeks. And he said, what are you doing here? And I said, what do you mean? What I am doing? I'm going to Israel like everybody else. He said, well, don't you know that your mother and sister are waiting for you? So no need to say that as much as I didn't want to set foot in Hungary ever again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just-- they were looking for me. They knew where I was. They knew where I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't reach me. They knew where I was. They couldn't get the news to me. And it was one week, exactly one week, before my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ship would have been ready to go from Italy to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I packed up and went back to Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was 1946. 1946, July. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went back to Hungary and I stayed there until 1949. Can you describe the reunion with your mother and sister? Where did you meet them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think there's any way to describe it because I can only say that where both of us were coming back from-- if you know what the Olam Haba is. From the other world we came back. And I certainly didn't think that they were alive. So I never expected to see them again. At least they knew that I was alive. But, yeah, I rather not go into the details. But all I can say is that it was a reunion which was bittersweet. It was bittersweet because out of the 17 people, there was only three of us who came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to say that your mother and your sister-- because they have passed on. Would you like to say something on their behalf about what they went through or something to one of their memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] no. I don't think so. I prefer not to go into it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What happened to me was one thing. I get emotional about it as little as possible. But it was me. But I rather don't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When and who did you marry? And why after that did you want to come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I wanted to come to Australia was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my main objective was to be away from Europe as fast as possible. I wanted to go to the furthest point on this Earth from Europe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was Australia. I could have gone to New Zealand, but it was Australia because I heard that other people came to Australia. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That is the truth. I'm not making any-- I'm not putting on any sort of honest intentions of why I came to Australia because I heard of good land or whatever. It is a beautiful country. It is a lovely country.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived on the 30th of January 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have three children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, just briefly, what effect has your suffering, the Shoah, had on the way you've brought them up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if it was my experience during the war, the Holocaust, which had that effect, or it would have been otherwise also the same way. But I always brought them [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as proud of being Jewish as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to finish tape five here.
